title: “‘Ignorance, ignorance,’ venerable sir, is said. What, venerable sir, is ignorance? And to what extent is one called ignorant?” - “Here, monk, an untaught ordinary…
content: “‘Ignorance, ignorance,’ venerable sir, is said. What, venerable sir, is ignorance? And to what extent is one called ignorant?” - “Here, monk, an untaught ordinary person does not know as it actually is: ‘Form is subject to origination’ as form is subject to origination; he does not know as it actually is: ‘Form is subject to cessation’ as form is subject to cessation; he does not know as it actually is: ‘Form is subject to origination and cessation’ as form is subject to origination and cessation. This, monk, is called ignorance. And to that extent one is called ignorant.”

title: “Monks, there are these two kinds of pleasure. What two? Sensual pleasure and the pleasure of renunciation. These, monks, are the two pleasures. The best…
content: “Monks, there are these two kinds of pleasure. What two? Sensual pleasure and the pleasure of renunciation. These, monks, are the two pleasures. The best of these two pleasures, monks, is the pleasure of renunciation.”

title: “And what, monks, are the defilements of the mind? Covetousness, unrighteous greed is a defilement of the mind; ill-will is a defilement of the mind;…
content: “And what, monks, are the defilements of the mind? Covetousness, unrighteous greed is a defilement of the mind; ill-will is a defilement of the mind; anger is a defilement of the mind; hostility is a defilement of the mind; hypocrisy, spite, envy, stinginess, deceit, fraud, obstinacy, impetuosity, conceit, arrogance, vanity, heedlessness are defilements of the mind.”

title: “One who once was heedless and later is not, Illumines the world like the moon freed from clouds.” “He who deeds of merit makes, And…
content:
“One who once was heedless and later is not,
Illumines the world like the moon freed from clouds.”

“He who deeds of merit makes,
And thereby his evil deeds dispels,
Illumines the world like the moon freed from clouds.”

“Blinded is this world,
Few are those who see clearly.
Like birds escaped from a net,
Few go to heaven.”

“Like a swan flying through the sky,
With psychic power soaring through space,
Having conquered Māra and his host,
The wise one leaves this world behind.”

title: Elder Nāgasamāla: Adorned, dressed in fine clothes, They set the bait for Death. Then I attended to it, Reflecting wisely — The danger became clear,…
content:
Elder Nāgasamāla:

Adorned, dressed in fine clothes,
They set the bait for Death.

Then I attended to it,
Reflecting wisely —
The danger became clear,
I dwelt in disenchantment.

And my mind was liberated.
Seeing the Dhamma as it is,
I attained the three true knowledges,
Accomplishing the Buddha’s teaching.

title: “Mahānāma, in the past, when I was still a bodhisatta, not yet fully enlightened, I saw with correct wisdom as it actually is: ‘Sensual pleasures…
content: “Mahānāma, in the past, when I was still a bodhisatta, not yet fully enlightened, I saw with correct wisdom as it actually is: ‘Sensual pleasures are of little satisfaction, much suffering, much despair, and the peril is even greater.’ Yet, even though I saw that with correct wisdom, I did not attain the rapture and pleasure born of seclusion, of non-sensuality, of unwholesome states, or anything higher than that. And then I knew that I was not yet free from being dominated by sensual pleasures. Mahānāma, when I saw with correct wisdom as it actually is: ‘Sensual pleasures are of little satisfaction, much suffering, much despair, and the peril is even greater,’ and I attained the rapture and pleasure born of seclusion, of non-sensuality, of unwholesome states, or anything higher than that, then I knew that I was free from being dominated by sensual pleasures.”

title: “Here, monks, a monk reflects, reflects thus: ‘The various sufferings of many kinds that arise in the world, such as aging and death — what…
content:
“Here, monks, a monk reflects, reflects thus: ‘The various sufferings of many kinds that arise in the world, such as aging and death — what is their cause, what is their origin, what gives them birth, what brings them into existence? When what exists does aging and death come to be? When what does not exist does aging and death not come to be?’

Reflecting thus, he understands: ‘The various sufferings of many kinds that arise in the world, such as aging and death — birth is their cause, birth is their origin, birth gives them birth, birth brings them into existence. When birth exists, aging and death come to be. When birth does not exist, aging and death do not come to be.’”

title: “Monks, there are these five strands of sensual pleasure. What five? Forms cognizable by the eye—desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. Sounds cognizable…
content: “Monks, there are these five strands of sensual pleasure. What five? Forms cognizable by the eye—desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. Sounds cognizable by the ear … Odors cognizable by the nose … Tastes cognizable by the tongue … Tangibles cognizable by the body—desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. These, monks, are the five strands of sensual pleasure. The pleasure and joy that arise dependent on these five strands of sensual pleasure—this is called sensual pleasure, defiled pleasure, worldling’s pleasure, ignoble pleasure. ‘I say that this kind of pleasure should not be pursued, should not be developed, should not be cultivated, should be feared.’”

title: “And what, monks, is non-restraint? Monks, there are forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable … enticing. If a monk delights in those forms,…
content: “And what, monks, is non-restraint? Monks, there are forms cognizable by the eye that are desirable … enticing. If a monk delights in those forms, praises them, and dwells with lust for them — then that monk, monks, should understand: ‘I have fallen away from wholesome states.’ The Tathāgata calls this ‘falling away.’ And, monks, there are sounds … odors … tastes … phenomena cognizable by the mind … If a monk delights in those phenomena, praises them, and dwells with lust for them — then that monk should understand: ‘I have fallen away from wholesome states.’ The Tathāgata calls this ‘falling away.’ Thus, monks, is non-restraint.”

title: “Udāyi, there are these five strands of sensual pleasure. What five? Forms cognizable by the eye—desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. Sounds cognizable…
content: “Udāyi, there are these five strands of sensual pleasure. What five? Forms cognizable by the eye—desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. Sounds cognizable by the ear … Odors cognizable by the nose … Tastes cognizable by the tongue … Tangibles cognizable by the body—desirable, agreeable, pleasant, charming, connected with sensuality, enticing. These, Udāyi, are the five strands of sensual pleasure. Whatever pleasure or joy arises in dependence on these five strands of sensual pleasure, that is called sensual pleasure, defiled pleasure, worldling’s pleasure, ignoble pleasure. I say that such pleasure should not be pursued, should not be developed, should not be cultivated, should be feared.”
